evidence_quotes.quote: prohibition of manipulative design features (dark patterns)
liability for unmarked or harmful influencer marketing
stricter regulation of personalisation of prices and advertising

paragraphs.text: The AVES Citizen’s Rights Association welcomes the European Commission’s proposal for a Digital Justice Act, which responds to proven shortcomings in consumer protection in the online environment. We fully support the objectives of the initiative, in particular: prohibition of manipulative design features (dark patterns), liability for unmarked or harmful influencer marketing, protection against addictive design of applications and digital services, stricter regulation of personalisation of prices and advertising, protection of minors as a horizontal priority. At the same time, we draw attention to a number of important practical aspects: 1. Slovak consumers are significantly under-informed – the issue of manipulative digital practices is virtually not known in Slovak media. There are no investigative articles or public campaigns to warn ordinary people of dark patterns, personalised manipulation or unlabelled advertising. This communication deficit results in: lack of awareness of the risks of digital behaviour, reduced ability to defend, complain or claim rights, less social support for enforcing these rules. In the final proposal, we recommend that the Commission: it supported Member States in educational and information activities, created a single European information portal with examples of unfair digital practices, strengthened the role of civil society in raising awareness. 2. Slovakia’s monitoring of influencer marketing confirms the problem – According to IAB Slovakia’s monitoring (Q3 2024), up to 29 % of the commercial contributions of Slovak influencers were not correctly labelled as advertising. This practice leads to a systematic misleading of the consumer. We therefore support the EC’s proposal to: mandatory use of tools such as paid partnerships, sanctions for unmarked advertising, liability of platforms hosting the advertisement. 3. Digital techniques, as confirmed by the practice of the BEUC (Shein, Temu), require legal instruments – from the practice of European organisations are known cases of dark patterns (Shein, Temu), which we believe should be specifically named and defined in legislation, e.g. deduction of remaining time, hidden possibilities of cancellation or confirm shaming. We consider the Commission’s proposal to be extremely necessary. At the same time, we stress that there will be no real change without more awareness-raising, more active national oversight and empowerment of consumer organisations.
